title: Bunny's Backlog Case Files
speaker: Guide

win.title: Case Closed!
win.subtitle: The Mystery of the Crust & Crumb Bakery

completeDialogue: Fantastic work, Rookie! You cracked the bakery break-in case using array iteration, custom comparator sorting logic, and functional try-catch exception tracking structures!
completeHint: All suspect files are sorted, verified, and accounted for.

phases.dialogue:
Thanks so much for helping me look after the office while I'm away, Rookie! ૮꒰˶ᵔ ᵕ ᵔ˶꒱ა
I actually left a little welcome gift for you! The bad news? I think I forgot to take it out of my super-secret locked drawer before I stepped out ૮꒰˶´ ꒳ `꒱ა. But don't worry, the key is hidden somewhere in the room!
I usually start all my mysteries by doing a quick self.search("room") what objects in the room I can interact with.
phases.output:
Looking around room I see a...

Found: plantPot

Found: lockedDrawer
phases.lesson: Use self.search("room") to list interactable targets inside a local physical boundary context.

hints.message: Almost! Use the string variable format: self.search("look around room")

phases.dialogue:
Sometimes a basic search isn't enough—we need to be way more specific! We can give our actions extra details by separating them with a comma.
For example, if I wanted to look behind a chair, I would type: self.search("behind", someChair).
But you have to be super careful! Our security system is incredibly sensitive... you could say it's case-sensitive ૮꒰ - ﻌ •꒱ა! Capital letters and lowercase letters matter immensely. Also, the order matters! The direction word in quotes always comes first, and the object variable comes second.
phases.output:
Searching under the plantPot...

Success! You found a brass key!
phases.lesson: Functions accept parameters separated by commas. String literals require quotes; object identifier variables do not.

hints.message: SyntaxError: plantPot is an object variable reference, remove the quotes around it!

phases.dialogue: The drawer is locked tight. Try using your unlock routine: self.unlock(target, tool)
phases.output:
🔴 ERROR: TypeError: Assignment to constant variable.

This object is a CONSTANT and cannot be changed!
phases.lesson: Constants declared with const lock value bindings. Reassignment parameters trigger a system runtime TypeError.

phases.dialogue:
Oh no! I completely forgot to mention our office security mechanism! ૮꒰˶´ ꒳ `꒱ა
To protect important office things from being tampered with, our security system sets them up using const. A variable made with const is totally unchangeable—meaning once it's set, its status stays that way forever! Look at your evidence page; that drawer's current state is locked down tight as a constant.
If we want a variable's status to actually change—like turning a drawer from locked to unlocked—we have to declare it using let. Using let 'lets' our system change its state!
Let's override the drawer's permanent setting so we can unlock it. Type this line to let it change:
phases.output: 🟢 SUCCESS: lockedDrawer status can now be changed!
phases.lesson: Variables declared with let are mutable and allow system actions to alter their properties or reference frames dynamically.

phases.dialogue: Okay, the security sequence updated. The drawer isn't a permanent constant anymore. Let's try that unlock command one more time!
phases.output: 🟢 SUCCESS: Click! The lock turns. The drawer is open!
phases.lesson: Altering variable constraints allows procedures to execute changes safely.

phases.answer: console.warn("I am just a lizard technician")
phases.dialogue: If you ever need to print a big warning message directly to the console screen to tell someone something important, use console.warn("your message")! ૮꒰˶ᵔ ᵕ ᵔ˶꒱ა
phases.hint: console.warn("I am just a lizard technician")
phases.output: ⚠️ WARNING: I am just a lizard technician
phases.lesson: console.warn() flags system runtime conditions visually inside output transcripts.

phases.dialogue:
When you have a big group of items to look through, our security console stores them in an Array. Think of an array like a numbered list, where every item on the list is called an element!
To look at every single item on the list one by one without missing anything, we use a tool called .forEach(). It lets us grab each individual element and check it out in order.
Try typing this command to start inspecting every dessert inside the display case array:
displayCase.forEach( dessert => { } )
phases.output: 🟢 SUCCESS: Ready to inspect elements inside displayCase!
phases.lesson: Arrays hold ordered listings. The array prototype method .forEach executes iterative operations on separate indices sequentially.

phases.dialogue:
Great job! When examining an array of objects, we also need to consider what exactly we're checking for. To ensure I don't forget, I list specific actions I want to perform in a { } after the for-each. To prevent confusion, I use a ; to indicate the end of an action!
Because the elements of an array are numbered, it's not easy to go and add a new element in between two others. This would require re-numbering every element that comes after! That's why I usually use array.push(newElement).
By pushing the new element onto the end, I don't change the order of the elements already on the list, saving me a lot of effort! Let's grab the name field and add it to our list:
phases.output:
Scanning display case elements...
Added to list: "Cinnamon Swirl"
Added to list: "Strawberry Tart"
Added to list: "Croissant"
🟢 SUCCESS: remainingDessertList updated!
phases.lesson: array.push() dynamically appends structural properties onto the terminal index boundary of target sequences.

phases.dialogue: Show the finished list to Baker Sheep by telling the logging console you are done.
phases.lesson: console.log output parameters map raw variable structures directly out to system interface buffers.

phases.dialogue:
Oftentimes I need to organize evidence for my cases, but sometimes using .sort() to organize things alphabetically isn't enough or right for the task.
In cases like these, I need to define my own sorting function to determine the relation of each piece to each other. We compare two items at a time—let's call them dessert1 and dessert2.
Then, when doing a sort, I can specify my own custom family sorting method like this:
remainingDessertList.sort((dessert1, dessert2) => dessert1.sheepOrdering(dessert2));
phases.output:
Applying .sheepOrdering() rules...
🟢 SUCCESS: remainingDessertList perfectly organized!
phases.lesson: The high-order functional argument inside .sort() passes pairs through evaluating comparators to restructure array index rankings.

phases.dialogue:
To bring dynamic items into a scene, we call our classic search command on the data folder we need.
Let's locate the main folder containing our records. Type this command to grab the profiles:
self.search(suspectFiles)
phases.lesson: Unquoted identifiers pass complex object states or directories into file search parameters.

phases.dialogue:
Sometimes I need to perform a repetitive task on similar objects over and over again. To help save time, I can write a function to prep how I will behave to some object.
Once I prepare a function like eatCarrot(someCarrot) { } by writing the actions I want to take inside { }, I can call the function over and over again on different objects without having to rewrite all my actions! Like eatCarrot(bigCarrot) or eatCarrot(smallCarrot).
someCarrot acts as a placeholder, so I can prepare and ready my actions before I want to perform them on the real thing! Let's start our file check framework:
function crossRef(suspect)
phases.lesson: Functions encapsulate programmatic procedures. Named arguments act as scoping definitions for evaluation routines.

phases.dialogue:
Excellent! Now that our function framework has a placeholder ready, the very first internal action we need to take is telling our device to pull up the record sheet.
We wrap our file reading step inside an experimental try routine. This safely attempts the behavior so that if something goes wrong, it won't crash our entire screen console.
Type this block inside the framework to begin:
try { self.readFile(suspect) }
phases.output: 🟢 SUCCESS: Try block created. Ready to process exceptions.
phases.lesson: The try runtime monitoring evaluation shell protects general script execution context environments against severe error flags.

phases.output: 🟢 SUCCESS: Exception handling compiled. All filters active!
phases.lesson: Catch statements receive explicit thrown reference structures. The ++ incremental operator adds mathematical units sequentially.

phases.dialogue:
We're almost done! Instead of writing heavy checks, we can dynamically insert our suspect right into a text string using the special form ${variableName} inside backticks.
Let's make our official declaration to log the suspect to the system:
console.log(`I accuse ${suspect3}`)
phases.output:
Evaluating suspect1 (Owl)... Total Contradictions: 2 (notBigEars, doesNotSqueak)
Evaluating suspect2 (Dog)... Total Contradictions: 1 (doesNotSqueak)
Evaluating suspect3 (Mouse)... Total Contradictions: 0 (Perfect Match!)
📢 LOG: I accuse suspect3
phases.lesson: Template literal blocks declared with backticks process embedded expressions dynamically via standard syntactic formatting notation ${}.
